questions.question: Explain sequential file organization with its advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Sequential organization stores records in consecutive order
Advantages: Simple, efficient for batch processing, compact storage
Disadvantages: Slow random access, difficult to update records in middle

questions.question: What is indexed file organization? Describe its structure and access methods.
keyPoints: Uses index file pointing to data blocks
Two-level indexing: tracks index points to primary blocks
Supports both sequential and random access
Example: ISAM (Indexed Sequential Access Method)

questions.question: Differentiate between primary storage and secondary storage.
keyPoints: Primary: volatile (RAM), fast access, directly accessed by CPU
Secondary: non-volatile (disk, SSD), slower, requires I/O operations
Primary used for currently running programs, secondary for permanent storage

questions.question: What is data redundancy? Explain the problems it causes in file systems.
keyPoints: Unnecessary duplication of same data in multiple files
Update anomaly: must update all copies
Insert anomaly: cannot add certain data without adding other data
Delete anomaly: losing one record deletes related information

questions.question: Describe the purpose and functioning of a buffer in file I/O operations.
keyPoints: Temporary memory area between program and disk
Reduces number of I/O operations by accumulating data
Block I/O: reads/writes chunks rather than individual records
Improves performance significantly for sequential access

questions.question: Explain the concept of file fragmentation and its impact on system performance.
keyPoints: File data scattered across non-contiguous disk locations
Occurs due to deletion and insertion of files
Increases disk seek time and rotational latency
Defragmentation reorganizes files contiguously

questions.question: What is the role of metadata in file systems? Give examples.
keyPoints: Data about data - describes file attributes
Examples: file name, size, creation date, permissions, location
Maintained in directory entries or inode structures
Essential for file management and access control
